title: Address Space Anatomy
description: Inspect address spaces, allocations, and job output. Destroy the process-centric mental model.

steps.title: What is an Address Space?
steps.screen:
z/OS Address Space Model

Not a process. Not a container.
An address space is a 64-bit virtual memory region.

Types:
- Started Tasks (STC) — system daemons
- TSO sessions — interactive users
- Batch jobs — JES-scheduled work
- System address spaces — JES2, RACF, VTAM

All share the same physical memory via DAT.
steps.expected: Understand that z/OS address spaces are not Unix processes.
steps.hint: Multiple address spaces can share memory via cross-memory services — a key attack surface.

steps.title: Display Active Address Spaces
steps.screen:
SDSF DA (Display Active)

NJobname  StepName ProcStep  Status   ASID  CPU
-JES2     JES2              ACTIVE   0001  12.3
-RACF     RACF              ACTIVE   0002  0.1
-VTAM     VTAM              ACTIVE   0003  0.4
-TSO      IKJEFT01          ACTIVE   0045  0.2
-HERC01   IKJEFT01          ACTIVE   0046  0.1

Command ===>
steps.expected: List all active address spaces including system tasks.
steps.hint: ASID (Address Space ID) is the unique identifier. JES2=0001, RACF=0002 are always present.

steps.title: TSO Session Address Space
steps.screen:
SDSF DA — Filter: HERC01

NJobname  StepName  Status   ASID  CPU   SIO
-HERC01   IKJEFT01  ACTIVE   0046  0.1   142

This is YOUR address space.
IKJEFT01 = TSO terminal monitor program.
All your commands run here under your RACF identity.
steps.expected: Identify your own TSO address space.
steps.hint: Your identity (HERC01) is bound to this address space for its entire lifetime.

steps.title: Cross-Memory Services
steps.screen:
z/OS Cross-Memory Architecture

Address spaces can communicate via:
- PC (Program Call) — authorized cross-memory calls
- XMEM (Cross-Memory Attach) — shared data
- SRB (Service Request Block) — async work units

Security implication:
A compromised address space with TRUSTED or PRIVILEGED
attribute can inject work into ANY other address space.
steps.expected: Understand cross-memory as an attack vector.
steps.hint: This is how mainframe rootkits work — inject an SRB into a privileged address space.

steps.title: Job Output Analysis
steps.screen:
SDSF OUTPUT — HERC01

NJobname  JobID    Owner  Prty  Queue  Dest
-HERC01   TSU00046 HERC01 15    PRINT  LOCAL

Browse output:
IKJ56455I HERC01 LOGGED ON AT 21:15:32
IKJ56951I NO BROADCAST MESSAGES
READY

Session log shows all commands executed.
steps.expected: TSO session output is logged and auditable via SMF.
steps.hint: SMF type 30 records capture all TSO command activity — this is the audit trail.

steps.title: Lab Complete
steps.screen:
Address Space Anatomy — Complete

Key concepts:
- Address spaces != Unix processes
- Identity is bound at logon, persists for session lifetime
- Cross-memory = lateral movement vector
- TRUSTED/PRIVILEGED = supervisor-state escalation
- SMF records all activity

Mental model shift:
Think in terms of identity planes, not process trees.
steps.expected: Replace the process-centric mental model with the address space model.
steps.hint: On z/OS, the attack graph is identity-centric, not process-centric.
